Got asked about User Stories in an interview and realized I wasn't crisp on it — so I looked it up, made it my own, and wrote down notes for later review.
There's a lot more to User Stories than this — just a primer.
Quick definition from the web — designing features or writing specs from the customer or user's perspective. Essentially: what does the user need? Implementation details come later.
Common examples you'll see in the wild:
Users can post a resume on the site
Companies can post new job listings
Users can limit who sees their resume
That's a bit abstract on its own — which is why people use a formula:
As a < type of user >, I want < some goal > so that < some reason >.
As a < role of user >, I want < some feature > so that < some business value >.
So if you're thinking about movie ticket booking, it might look like:
As a movie fan, I want to be able to grab tickets from my phone in real time, so that I can get to the theater and walk straight into the movie.
As a job seeker on Indeed, I want a place to upload my resume, so that I have a better chance of getting more job opportunities.

User Stories are a user-oriented spec format — different from how a backend dev might naturally approach the same problem. Personally, when I'd planned systems before this, I'd usually start with "what tables and columns does this need?" The User Story angle is the opposite — start from the user's intent. Different mindset. Takes practice to get used to.

An interviewer asked me about User Stories and I realized I wasn't super clear on it — so I dug in and wrote a short note. A User Story captures a feature from the user's perspective ("As a [user], I want [something] so that [reason]") rather than from a database or implementation perspective. This post is the basics — the formula, a few real examples, and how it shifts how you think about building features.
